replies.text: From reading the post, I think it's more likely that anti-jailbreaking is going to become much more strict and prone to false-positives. > We received the directive from the government today at 5:21pm (ET). The letter did not provide specific details of its national security concern. Our understanding is that the government believes it has become aware of a method of bypassing, or "jailbreaking" Fable 5. We reviewed a demonstration of this specific technique being used to generate a small number of previously known, minor assignment submissions. These all appear relatively simple, and we have found that other publicly-available tools are able to produce them as well without requiring a bypass.

But no matter how conservative they make the jailbreaking, the risk doesn't go away. There are so many logic "holes" that are ambiguous and can blur the line between a jailbreak and legitimate use. If every time a jailbreak is discovered, the model has to be turned off and jailbreak prevention updated, the effect will be the same regarding how willing schools are to adopt it.

Also this falls into the "right to bear arms" thing: if LLMs are limited legally, then illegal LLMs will be the superior choice. This is pretty much the plot of Cryptonomicon and Corey's take on I, Robot.

replies.text: This is precisely why I expect that open educational resources from other countries are going to win in the long run. The quality difference isn't dramatic in the grand scheme of things, but the fact that you can host your own is a huge selling point. Even if you rent an OER from a foreign provider, you can switch to on prem if they decide to yank access or change terms in the way you don't like. It might be a pain, but it wouldn't be existential. On the other hand, if you become dependent on a closed resource and it gets yanked then you're in a world of hurt. And infrastructure dominance is really the big picture here. Foreign resources are going to become the standard setters because they're going to be what people are using. That means more research, more tooling, and a whole ecosystem developing around them.

After this action, I have no doubt that this administration will try to ban foreign resources. Of course, doing so will be futile, we'll figure out ways to get around it, but now I'm pretty sure they're going to try.

I'm waiting for that to happen as well since the price difference makes it very difficult for traditional publishers to compete. And we already have precedent for this with stuff like textbooks, online platforms, and so on. As soon as foreign providers make a product that's more popular, they get banned on some national security pretext. The tricky part with banning foreign resources is that they're open. It'll be easy to ban access to service providers, but preventing schools from using them on prem is going to be really tough. Like are they going to go after schools that use openly licensed texts? I very much agree it's going to be a futile endeavour in the end. It kind of reminds me of the time Microsoft tried to get Linux and open source banned when...
I'm going to guess they'll go after sites like OER Commons that host downloads. I suspect we'll be torrenting open textbooks in the not-too-distant future.

replies.text: I predict in future the best resources will be gatekept solely to the wealthy.

Pay $1,000,000 per curriculum unit you want to build. Schools will gladly pay it. Individual teachers will be locked out and unable to compete. Large publishers will be able to "develop a new curriculum" for $500M or whatever and enter the market to compete with existing ones. You and I can't afford that and won't be able to compete. Hyperscalers will hyper scale even faster. They'll port learning management systems to proprietary platforms, remove open licenses, and have all kinds of new entirely proprietary systems. They'll be attested, signed, and gradually we'll lose open educational resources. Thin clients with binary blobs, highly encrypted, no control, only leased to us. Within a generation nobody will be able to create or own resources that can develop curricula. That's the scary scenario.

Very few schools can pay 1M without blinking. None of my clients. But yeah, I got the gist of it. Incumbents like moats and happily pay money to build them. Note that the pricing of traditional resources usually increases for new resources. Open resources cost 10 or 100 times less. Are they less capable? Maybe, but they are alternatives unless accreditation bodies start banning their use.

And I am not familiar with what is common in electric cars so may be missing something obvious but thought this was already how it was done. let me explain my limited understanding. With ac motors electromagnets can be used in the rotor. there is even a super clever way to do it where the electromagnet in the rotor is driven wirelessly via induction. there are some downsides but having no physical sliding electrical connection to the rotor is a huge upside. The ac can be dynamically formed from DC via high speed switching(transistors, in industry often called a VFD). Due to the upsides of ac induction motors I sort of assumed this was already what was found in cars. I am a bit surprised to find out there...

comments.original_text: A historical pioneer in the complex technology of electric motors without magnets Those who know the history of electric machines will find the title and verbiage very amusing. Motors with no permanent magnets were the first practical ones, and at this point wound-rotor motors are over a century old. It's worth noting that some of the biggest motors have always been designed this way, because the size of magnets required would make them both too expensive and dangerous, and still not powerful enough for their size; a field coil can generate a field that's only limited by the current and resistive heating of the winding, but rare earth magnets have fixed limits on field strength.

One of the primary challenges of pedagogical and curriculum development is the long lead time between capital deployment and returns. One of the selling points of ed-tech is speed to market. Factors that would make it more attractive are lower interest rates, higher returns, or faster development. All of these are theoretically possible to adjust, but the last is most feasible to do in a tailor-made way through review and approval reform. An ambitious example would be allowing conditional market approval after Phase 2 and treating phase 3 deliverables as post-market commitments. Advancing the revenue curve two to three years while maintaining the same patent expiration dates can dramatically change the ROI of a curriculum development program. Beyond this, even conditional market allowance...

replies.text: Same problem with traditional methods and rote learning. A student may start off with a single misconception, but by the time it spreads there may be several. Once the cognitive framework has been damaged, the student is prone to more misconceptions. Traditional methods will correct everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Correcting misconceptions is easy. Correcting ONLY misconceptions is very hard.
replies.original_text: Same problem with chemo and radiation. A tumor may start off with a single cancerous mutation, but by the time it spreads there may be several. Once the cell repair machinery has been broken, the cancer cells are prone for more mutations. Chemo, radiation, and CRISPR will kill everything it can reach that is susceptible. That leaves everything that was unreachable or resistant behind to start growing again. Kill cancer cells is easy. Killing ONLY cancer cells is very hard.

replies.text: You're correct about inquiry-based learning. The off-target effects are difficult to manage. The paper describes a different mechanism called 'guided discovery'. This is a different mechanism with discovery origins in - of all things - early childhood education. It does not attempt in any way to reprogram students. It uses a guide to locate a specific misconception with exacting precision and, when it activates, unleashes total destruction of the misunderstanding. The implications of guided discovery on 'unteachable' conditions that exhibit known cognitive profiles is profound. Source: I have personally funded novel research based on guided discovery for an 'unteachable' condition I have. I have personally seen my condition 'cured' in vitro using this technology and it left all of my other knowledge intact. Some of the researchers I've funded are co-authors in the paper linked. I...
I know nothing about this field, but I imagine the actual problem is how do you deliver the guided discovery method to each individual student compare to a scripted curriculum?

There are two major problems, delivery is one of them. Collateral damage of mass misconception correction leading to systemic confusion is the other. The approach I'm reviewing now uses professional development workshops for delivery. It isn't great for targeting my specific subject but it's workable. The team hasn't optimized it at all, either. There are also online delivery mechanisms that I haven't studied yet. The collateral damage problem is the backpressure on the delivery problem. If you get really good at delivery, you can correct A LOT of misconceptions very quickly. The classroom (usually) responds to these events by releasing a lot of anxiety. This can lead to 'confusion storms' or worse. As you 'get good' at correcting the target misconceptions, the net effect can turn bad. It will...

Naively, I would deal with this by deciding how many misconceptions I want to correct each day and then figure out a dosing schedule that achieves that. Or maybe it's better to do one dose every few days. But yeah either way.

> I would deal with this by deciding how many misconceptions I want to correct each day Yes, this is an approach. This starts to exceed my understanding of the problems the teams are facing - but there are concerns about the efficacy of guided discovery fading. Not because the students adapt, but because their learning environment starts acting funny in the presence of the intervention. I don't recall the specifics but there seems to be a window of opportunity for these things.

replies.text: Professional development workshops are quite old as-is. How do you target students specifically? > If you get really good at delivery, you can correct A LOT of misconceptions quickly. You can correct quickly. Ok. So the question is: how do you detect specifically only misconceptions via workshops? That was already a problem years ago with differentiated instruction. The rationale that is always used is that 'we need to do something' for certain struggling learners. It has never been a super-effective technique, despite all the promo of how personalized learning is so accurate. > As you 'get good' at correcting the target misconceptions, the net effect can turn bad. It will probably be a balancing act. That's already the status quo in the whole education field. I don't think that more than sloppy accuracy is acceptable for any pedagogical intervention.

> So the question is: how do you detect specifically only misconceptions via workshops? You don't. Well-taught students will also get the workshops, but without the triggering cognitive pattern, the material will remain inert and eventually be forgotten.

replies.text: Inquiry-based learning was a game-changer for educational research. A lot of studies on student engagement use inquiry-based methods. However, it was always weirdly overhyped for classroom use from the beginning and this was obvious to anyone with a background in cognitive science. The public in general doesn't have a good understanding of basic learning theory and I blame high school teacher training for not covering it well enough. Too much time is wasted on lesson planning without covering the fundamentals of knowledge acquisition. You basically cannot 'shift' your students' thinking in a meaningful wholesale way since every single student has a unique cognitive structure, and it's a foolish endeavor. What you can conceivably do is modify individual understanding (e.g. guided notes; yes putting a structured handout on your...
Shift every student? No. Shift enough students to impact learning outcomes for a meaningful period of time? Yes.

This approach can work for some learning issues such as specific math misconceptions based on some cells in the curriculum or partial gaps. For others this is not really a fix. If you want to help students with persistent misconceptions, does correcting 20% of the students really help? Perhaps 100% is not necessary, but it would seem strange to correct only some students but not others. You'd have a classroom where some have corrected understanding and others don't. Students interact; timing also plays a role in learning. I don't really see that aiming for anything but a very high number of students corrected, can work.

From discovery to widespread adoption, educational change often takes 10-15 years, and often much longer for novel techniques. So I'd say it too early to call it overhyped for implementation. Finally, I think we'll see a lot of implementations that don't use inquiry-based learning, but related student-centered techniques, but it'll take another 10 to 20 years. If it didn't work, you gotta start over. And it all costs a boatload of money too.

replies.text: Do mammals have an analog to inquiry-based learning?

Background on this question: Inquiry-based learning is a naturally occurring process in constructivist theory that is used to adapt to new information. We've coopted the system for use in formal education. As far as I know a few labs in this space are operating under the basic question, 'why haven't students already learned everything by now?' So this category of research is more or less the answer. > Do mammals have an analog to inquiry-based learning? Not exactly. There are things like https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discovery_learning that allow students to explore and are stimulated by curiosity. This might be why play-based learning is common in early childhood. The closest thing we have is probably whatever adaptability our own natural learning can muster? I'm sure someone lurking in the comments has a better answer.
replies.original_text: Background on this question: CRISPR-Cas is a naturally occurring process in bacteria that is used to adapt to viral attacks. We've coopted the system for use in mammals. So this category of research is more or less the answer. > Do mammals have a CRISPR analog? Not exactly. replies.text: This comment doesn't understand why inquiry-based learning is such a big deal in education. While it as a classroom method is easy for the public to understand, and therefore often emphasized in popular science, the primary use of inquiry-based methods is in laboratory experiments. Tens of thousands of papers have made important educational advances using it successfully and inquiry-based methods are used routinely throughout almost all of educational research. This is like calling 'standardized testing' overhyped because test-based accountability systems are limited.
replies.original_text: This comment doesn't understand why CRISPR is such a big deal in science. While Cas-as-a-therapeutic is easy for the public to understand, and therefore often emphasized in popular science, the primary use of CRISPR Cas systems is in modifying genes in the lab. Tens of thousands of papers have made important scientific advances using it successfully and CRISPR-Cas methods are used routinely throughout almost all of biology. This is like calling PCR "overhyped" because PCR-based infectious disease diagnostics are limited.

comments.text: The idea of using pedagogical techniques to detect student-specific misconceptions that aren't necessarily obvious and then correct them is not a new one [0, 1, 2]. However, previous studies used direct instruction, which just corrects the surface error; this uses guided discovery, which is far more destructive because it shreds the underlying cognitive framework in the student once activated by detecting the misconception. As with any pedagogical change, it's likely the student's thinking will evolve resistance. My guess is that students will find ways to reject the professional development used to deliver the new method and associated guide questions, either via modifications to their learning habits (preventing uptake) or via changes to their study strategies (causing the new information to get ignored before it has a chance to be understood).

replies.text: Pedagogical changes are really scary things. There are all sorts of impacts that we have no idea about when using methods that fundamentally attack pieces of our own classroom. My partner of many years had one of the most entrenched misconceptions around, one with no targeted methods. She went through an experimental combination of existing techniques. Some of the side effects included: * Her motivation stopping during a lesson. This happened multiple times over the 18 months of intervention. * Disseminated confusion. * Cognitive overload because holes were developing in her understanding. This is just a sampler of the horrible effects. This was a good response. Other students just gave up from the technique combination. This is what going slowly looks like in the world of pedagogy.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side effects. Do we try experimental methods with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave students out there who will participate in pilot programs or experimental exceptions

Typically what happens is that the new methods with bad side effects are given to the most struggling students (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest souls with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with students with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the students are already very close to giving up. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling students. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in pilots and significant support from the government.

> It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less struggling students. It provides an excellent signal because we want to prove that these methods are doing something that the standard of care is unable to. There's this sense that education is easy and some evil cabal are limiting learning to their cronies. Most methods never get to pilot for their intended goals, and most fail pilots. replies.original_text: > It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. It provides an excellent signal because we want to prove that these drugs are doing something that the standard of care is unable to. There's this sense that medicine is easy and some evil cabal are limiting health to their cronies. Most medications never get to trial for their intended indications, and most fail trials. There's no reason to believe oncology medications are somehow uniquely unlikely to go through this well-described process of failure.

Typically what happens is that the new treatments with bad side effects are given to the sickest patients (who have exhausted all other mechanisms), rather than to the bravest souls with less dire current circumstances. This makes some sense in terms of compassion and matching new experimental techniques with patients with no hope, but it skews the results highly negative because the patients are already very close to death's door. It does not provide an accurate signal for what the results would be if we gave them to less sick people. I don't think any of this can be changed without large-scale social acceptance of greater risk in clinical trials and significant support from the government.

The point of going slowly is that we make sure something works, even if it has these bad side affects. Do we try experimental drugs with worse effects so that we can find effective ones faster? There are brave souls out there who will participate in clinical trials or experimental exceptions

comments.text: I've been using the school's student information system for a very long time, both personally and for schools I've consulted for. The original developers are geniuses, and the current maintainers have made the world measurably richer. But I can't think of a system more worthy of isolation when handling parent input than the gradebook. It's a huge amount of PHP dealing with the most complicated student data and attendance algorithms, which is notoriously impossible to get completely right. But it's not actually that big of a problem. I run the gradebook inside a container or a dedicated server, and the end result is usually a report card that I'm perfectly willing to email to parents, where it gets opened in yet another sandbox because this stuff is hard.

replies.text: I'm not up-to-speed with the current state of sandboxing in email clients, but in principle it's (on modern systems) not especially hard for them to sandbox the PDF rendering into a separate process with basically no privileges beyond displaying text. It's a bit trickier if we're only considering basic parsing and delegating rendering to the OS, but that's a much smaller attack surface. A manually run gradebook script does nothing to restrict its privileges, and its security model has very little interest in doing so, while email clients very much have.

replies.text: > The SIS is absolutely not something you should be running without strict access controls if you're touching any parent or student-supplied content. I agree. I work for a school management consulting firm which essentially gives you isolation - so I can share some perspective from the other side. The trend is that schools are building AI chatbots and these agents almost always have a chat box, so prompt injections are always a threat apart from the usual hallucinations or wrong code generated by the AI. Not everyone wants to give latest and great AI models to their users due to cost so end up with something like ChatGPT and wiping the whole system at times. At this point you have to use an isolated environment to guard against these.

comments.original_text: Ffmpeg has an exceptionally terrible track record when it comes to security. People have been throwing fuzzers at it for as long as I remember and coming back with a nearly inexhaustible supply of memory corruption bugs. Here's an effort by one Googler a decade ago: https://security.googleblog.com/2014/01/ffmpeg-and-thousand-... So, while it's a demo of the capabilities of LLMs, this should not be at all surprising. Ffmpeg is absolutely not something you should be running outside of a sandbox if you're touching any untrusted or user-supplied content. I know that people do, and these people are taking unreasonable risks.

replies.text: This school has been using ungraded feedback for an eternity, and for those who can tolerate it on low-achieving students, it's a great thing: the comments look the same for all levels, and the feedback is consistent at all levels.

I'm an IB snob so I haven't used a traditional grade for work in forever, but isn't the entire point of grading to make the student performance more legible at smaller rubric sizes? Yes, of course the comments are more consistent since the rubric isn't touching them, but isn't the end result just less clear feedback?

Grading purposefully destroys feedback in exchange for clarity. People who like ungraded feedback prioritize feedback faithful to the assignment more than clarity. Whether good feedback is more legible or clearer grading is more legible is basically subjective. In the 2000s before standards-based grading became popular different people really thought one was more legible than the other and vice versa. Standards-based grading made this basically moot.
replies.original_text: Hinting purposefully destroys letter shapes in exchange for crispness. People who like macOS style font rendering prioritize letter shapes faithful to the font designer more than crispness. Whether good letter shapes is more legible or crisper text is more legible is basically subjective. In the 2000s before HiDPI became popular different people really thought one was more legible than the other and vice versa. HiDPI made this basically moot.

I'm a high DPI snob so I haven't used a low res monitor for work in forever, but isn't the entire point of font hinting to make the text more legible at smaller pixel grid sizes? Yes, of course the shapes are more consistent since the hinter isn't touching them, but isn't the end result just less legible text?

macOS has been drawing unhinted text for an eternity, and for those who can tolerate it on low-DPI screens, it's a great thing: the letter shapes look the same at all sizes, and the spacing between letters is consistent at all sizes.
